  - Fragile boxes go on top and toward the back of the truck or in a clearly marked “fragile” area that will be unloaded first.

8. Fragile items, plants, and last loads
  - Load fragile art, mirrors, and boxes last so they’re on top and can be unloaded first. Use cardboard, blankets, and vertical crating for framed pieces.
  - Plants should be carried separately in your car if possible; if loaded, they should be last and near ventilation/avoid crushing—secure them so they won’t tip.

9. Secure everything
  - Use ratchet straps to anchor bulky items to the truck’s tie-down points. Cross straps where possible to prevent shifting.
  - Fill gaps with soft items (pillows, towels, clothes) to prevent movement.
  - Don’t leave voids where items can shift; use rolled blankets or crumpled cartons to pack voids.

10. Final checks and safe driving tips
  - Walk the load to ensure nothing is leaning or top-heavy.
  - Close and latch the rear door, then check straps periodically during stops.
  - Drive cautiously (slower turns, longer braking distance) with a loaded truck.

A few special notes for your situation
- Because you have a third‑floor walk‑up and narrow stairs, make sure the truck has a ramp or use a furniture dolly. But remember, ramp only helps curb→truck loading; you still need muscle/crew for stairs.
- If the sectional or fridge can be moved on a dolly and tilted safely, that reduces stair strain—use appliance straps with experienced helpers.
- Keep your essentials, valuables, cat carrier, and important documents with you in your car—not on the truck.

If you want, tell me your sectional’s exact largest dimensions and whether it breaks into pieces and I’ll give a tighter recommendation between 10/15/20' and suggest how to orient the sectional and fridge in the truck.

user: Pets: any advice for keeping my cat calm and safe from start to finish? Should I consider a sitter or a carrier in a quiet room during loading? | assistant: Good question — moving is very stressful for cats, but a little planning cuts risk and helps them stay calmer. Below are practical, safety‑focused steps and options so you can choose what fits your move.

Before moving day
- Acclimate to the carrier: leave the carrier out with soft bedding, treats, and toys so your cat sees it as a safe spot. Do short practice car rides if possible.
- Update ID: make sure collar tags and microchip contact info are up to date and include a secondary phone number. Bring a recent photo of your cat in case they escape.
- Vet check & records: get a copy of vaccination and medical records to carry with you. Ask your vet about motion‑sickness, anxiety, or short‑term sedatives if your cat is extremely stressed — do not use medication without vet advice.
- Prep a “cat kit”: carrier with comfy bedding, leash/harness (if used), 1–2 days’ food, travel water bowl, litter box or disposable tray, scoop, small litter, favorite toys, any meds, towel, and vet contact info.

Move‑day options (carrier vs sitter/boarding)
- Carrier in a quiet room (recommended for most)
  - Pros: safest for preventing escape and injury; you control environment.
  - How: put the cat in a single secured, quiet room with the door closed well before movers arrive. Put familiar bedding, litter, water, and food inside. Place a “DO NOT OPEN — CAT INSIDE” sign on the door. Tell movers the cat is secured and confirm they won’t open that door.
  - For loading: keep the cat in the room or in a closed carrier in your car — do NOT leave cat loose in an area with movers coming and going.
- Sitter / boarding (consider if your cat/or you prefer)
  - Sitter/boarding pros: removes cat from chaotic environment, no risk of escape during loading.
  - Sitter/boarding cons: travel to sitter/boarding may itself be stressful; some cats do better at home in a quiet room.
  - If using a sitter: arrange for them to take the cat offsite the day of the move (or have them stay at your place and care for the cat in the quiet room while movers work).
  - If boarding at a reputable facility/vet: book early, bring records, and confirm pickup/dropoff windows.

During loading and transit
- Keep cat secure in carrier: always transport the cat in a closed carrier. Avoid carriers that are flimsy; hard carriers or well‑padded soft carriers with secure latches are best.
- Cover the carrier with a towel/blanket to reduce visual stress but ensure ventilation.
- Secure the carrier in your car with a seatbelt or place on the floor behind front seat so it won’t shift. Never leave your cat unattended in a parked car.
- Avoid feeding immediately before travel if your cat tends to vomit (ask your vet for a recommended fasting window), but bring small snacks for breaks.
- Keep the carrier away from loud noises and direct sun in the car. Talk soothingly to your cat if it helps.

At the new apartment — first hours
- Set up a single “safe room” first: set up litter box, food, water, bedding, hiding spots, and a scratching post in one quiet room. Put familiar-smelling items and some unwashed bedding from the old place to comfort them.
- Confine for 24–72 hours: keep the cat in the safe room while you set up the rest of the apartment so they don’t bolt into unknown hallways or under heavy furniture. Let them explore gradually by opening the door and supervising initial steps.
- Reintroduce gradually: once they are relaxed in the safe room, allow short supervised explorations into new areas, then expand access slowly over a few days.
- Keep routine: feed and play on your usual schedule to reduce anxiety and help them settle.

Calming aids and environmental tricks
- Pheromone products: Feliway diffuser in the new apartment or spray in the carrier/room can reduce stress.
- Comfort items: familiar bedding, toys, and unwashed clothing with your scent help.
- Treats and play: gentle play and treats help them re‑associate the new place positively.
- Supplements/meds: OTC calming treats and supplements exist; for prescriptions or stronger anxiolytics, consult your vet.

Safety tips with movers and during handling
- Clearly label the cat’s room and tell movers a cat is inside and the room must remain closed.
- Keep windows and doors closed during loading/unloading and supervise any door openings.
- Don’t rely on movers to watch pets unless explicitly agreed and you’ve vetted them; you’re best to manage the cat or hire a sitter.

Emergency prep
- Research a vet near the new place and have their contact info in your cat kit.
- Carry a basic first‑aid kit and any current meds with you.
- If your cat escapes, call local shelters and post on neighborhood social apps and microchip registries.

Bottom line
- For most cats: confine them to a quiet room with all necessities or have a friend/sitter take them offsite during move day. Use a carrier for transit and set up a safe room at the new place for the first 24–72 hours. Consult your vet if your cat has severe anxiety to discuss medication or other options.

If you want, tell me whether your cat is carrier‑trained or extremely anxious and I can suggest a short prep plan (timeline and specific calming products) for the week before moving.

user: I have some plants — are there transport tips to keep them alive in transit and how to minimize mess in the truck? | assistant: Good — plants can survive moves with a little prep. Below are practical, step‑by‑step tips to keep them healthy in transit and to prevent dirt/water mess in the truck.

Before moving day (prepare)
- Prune and tidy: trim dead leaves/stems and remove loose soil debris so plants are compact and less messy.
- Inspect for pests/disease: treat infestations and isolate sick plants. Moving spreads pests quickly.
- Consider potting adjustments: if plants are in heavy ceramic pots, temporarily repot into lightweight nursery pots or plastic pots to reduce weight and breakage risk. Keep originals if they’re valuable and move them empty.
- Water timing: water plants 1–2 days before the move so soil is moist but not soggy. Avoid watering the morning of the move to reduce leaks.
- Stake and secure top growth: stake tall or floppy plants and wrap loosely with soft ties or a sheet to keep foliage contained.
- Group by tolerance: note which plants are heat/cold sensitive so you can transport them in the car instead of the truck if needed.

Packing and minimizing mess
- Use trays/saucers: place each pot on a saucer or shallow tray to catch any drainage. For small pots, a baking sheet works fine.
- Double‑bag pots: line a plastic bag (garbage bag or nursery bag) in the box, put the pot inside, fold the bag over the top of the soil and tape gently around the pot base to contain spills. For ceramic pots you’re keeping, put the pot in a larger plastic bag instead of putting soil in the bag.
- Boxing: put plants in sturdy boxes sized to keep them upright. Cut tall boxes lower or support with stakes. Pack soft packing material (towels, crumpled paper) around pots to prevent tipping.
- Use plant crates for larger plants: secure larger pots with straps or rope and surround with moving blankets. Don’t stack plants.
- Cover foliage: use a light sheet, paper, or breathable fabric—avoid plastic directly on leaves for extended periods because it can trap moisture and cause rot.
- Protect delicate leaves: wrap fragile leaves in paper or bubble wrap (not tightly).
- Absorbent layer: put old towels/newspapers in the bottom of the box/tray to absorb any spilled water.
- Tape saucers in place: if a saucer is used inside a box, tape it lightly to the box floor to prevent sliding.

Transport placement & handling
- Car vs truck: if possible, transport fragile or temperature‑sensitive plants in your car (passenger area) where you can control temperature and avoid extreme heat/cold. Truck is fine for hardy plants if properly secured.
- Load order: load plants last onto the vehicle and unload first at the new place so they aren’t crushed and spend minimal time in the vehicle.
- Secure upright: keep pots upright and avoid tipping. Use straps or boxes to secure them so they won’t shift.
- Temperature control: avoid leaving plants in a hot truck/van during stops. Shade them and ventilate. In cold weather, protect tropicals from freezing with blankets and keep them in the car.
- Short distances: protect from wind; windburn dries leaves quickly. Use closed vehicle or shield with blankets.

During transit
- Keep them shaded and ventilated. Don’t leave plants in a closed car/truck in high heat.
- Check trays/bags for leaks during stops and blot up spills.

At the new place (first 24–72 hours)
- Unload first and place in a sheltered, stable spot out of direct sun until they acclimate.
- Check soil moisture and water if soil feels dry (they may need less after transit).
- Wait a few days before fertilizing or repotting—let plants recover from stress first.
- Quarantine new location: keep plants separate until you confirm no pests/disease were transferred.

Supplies checklist
- Sturdy boxes or plant crates, saucers/trays, nursery pots (optional)
- Garbage bags / nursery bags, tape, scissors
- Towels/newspaper for padding and absorbing spills
- Stakes/soft plant ties, small trowel
- Moving blankets for large pots, straps or rope
- Plastic or corrugated cardboard for added protection

Pest & legal note
- If moving across state lines or into areas with strict agriculture rules, check local/state regulations (some states restrict moving soil or certain plants). For moves across borders, look up USDA/APHIS or the state department of agriculture guidance.

Quick checklist (day-of)
- Water 1–2 days before move (not the morning of).
- Put pots on saucers and double‑bag if boxing.
- Stake/secure tall plants and cover foliage lightly.
- Load plants last and secure upright.
- Unload first and place in a sheltered spot at the new home.

If you want, tell me how many plants and what types (succulents, tall tropicals, large pots) and I can suggest which ones to put in the car vs truck and specific packing setups.

user: What paperwork should I have ready for handover at new place (IDs, lease paperwork, proof of renter's insurance) and should I photograph the apartment on move-in? | assistant: Bring these items to the handover and be prepared to use them during the move‑in walkthrough. Keep originals on you and have digital copies (phone photos/PDFs) ready to share.

Documents and paperwork to have
- Photo ID (driver’s license or passport) — required for signing and key pickup.
- Signed lease and any addenda (bring one clean copy for your records if landlord will not).
- Proof of payment for move‑in charges (rent, security deposit, pet deposit, fees) — receipts, bank transfer screenshot, or cashier’s check stub.
- Proof of renters insurance (declarations page or emailed PDF showing policy number and effective date).
- Contact info for your employer/guarantor if requested (and any guarantor paperwork).
- Pet paperwork (vaccination records, registration, approved pet addendum) if applicable.
- Any required move‑in forms from the landlord or management (parking permit forms, move‑in scheduling receipts, key/fob authorization).
- Utility account or transfer confirmations (if landlord requires proof you’ve set up service).
- Emergency contact info (some buildings ask for this for files).

What to get/confirm at handover
- Keys, fobs, remotes, mailbox key, parking passes — count and note serial numbers or IDs.
- A signed move‑in condition/inspection form from the landlord or building rep (if they offer one).
- Contact information for building manager/maintenance and emergency numbers.
- Any promised repairs or agreed changes documented in writing, with expected completion timeline.
- Copies or instructions for trash/recycling rules, parking, package delivery, and building access procedures.

Move‑in inspection and paperwork steps
- Do a walk‑through with the landlord or agent if possible. Point out and document pre‑existing damage so it’s on record.
- Ask the landlord to sign or initial the move‑in condition form (or at least sign an emailed acknowledgment).
- Record start meter readings (gas/electric/water) and photograph them with date/time visible.

Should you photograph the apartment on move‑in?
Yes — absolutely. Photos and a short dated video walkthrough are one of the best defenses against unfair deposit deductions.

Practical photo/video tips
- Time‑stamp or keep originals with metadata (phone photos/videos keep that automatically).
- Photograph every room from multiple angles: wide shots and close‑ups of corners, floors, ceilings, walls, baseboards.
- Photograph appliances (inside & out), faucets, shower/tub, toilets, windowsills, door frames, locks, HVAC vents, thermostat, and any existing scratches/stains.
- Photograph meter readings, keys/fobs, and any signed paperwork handed to you.
- For suspected problem areas, take close-ups plus a wider shot to show context/scale.
- Record a video walkthrough while narrating (“living room, mark on west wall — scuff about 3 inches”) so you have time‑stamped audio confirmation of issues.
- Upload photos/videos to cloud storage (Google Drive, iCloud, Dropbox) and send a copy to the landlord/management via email within 24–48 hours asking them to confirm receipt. Keep the originals on your device as backup.

After handover (what to do next)
- Get a signed or emailed copy of any documents you signed that day (lease, inspection report, receipts).
- Confirm utility transfer was successful and that renter’s insurance policy is active on the lease start date.
- If landlord didn’t do a joint inspection, email your photo/video packet to the landlord and request written acknowledgment that those are the pre‑existing conditions.
- Save all documentation (photos, emails, receipts) in a folder for deposit disputes or future reference.

Quick checklist to bring with you
- Photo ID
- Signed lease & copies
- Proof of payment(s)
- Proof of renters insurance
- Pet/vaccine records (if applicable)
- Phone with charged battery + cloud backup for photos
- Small flashlight for dark closets/ceilings
- Pen for signatures and a notepad for notes

Following these steps and documenting thoroughly will protect you and make the move‑in handover smooth.

user: If I need to arrange curbside parking for the moving truck or coordinate dolly use with the building, what's the right way to handle that with management and neighbors? | assistant: Good — here’s a practical, ordered plan to handle curbside parking and dolly use with building management and neighbors, plus sample messages you can adapt.

When to start
- Contact building management 2–4 weeks before move to request loading zone/curb use and any building-specific move rules.
- If you need a municipal parking/loading permit or temporary “No Parking” signs, apply 1–2 weeks before (some cities need more lead time).
- Notify neighbors 3–7 days before as a courtesy and again the day before.

Steps to coordinate with management
1. Email or visit management early with details:
  - Move date, start time and estimated duration, truck length, number of movers, and whether you’ve hired pros or plan to DIY.
  - Explain it’s a third-floor walk-up and you’ll likely need curb-to-stair access and hall/stair protection.
  - Ask whether the building provides: an in‑building dolly, floor/banister protection, move-in staff assistance, preferred loading route, and where moving vehicles should park.
  - Offer proof of movers’ insurance and ask if management requires a copy or a damage deposit.
  - Ask about any fees, forms, or specific time windows and whether they require a move-in checklist or signed waiver.

2. Get written confirmation
  - Get management’s approval and any rules in writing (email is fine). Note assigned loading zones, move time restrictions, and any required supervision.

3. Arrange building protection
  - Agree on required protective measures (moving blankets, corner guards, floor runners or plywood). Determine who provides them (movers often provide; building may supply or require specific materials).

4. Ask about building equipment / dolly policy
  - If building has a furniture dolly, clarify availability, sign‑out procedure, weight limits, and if you must return it immediately after.
  - If internal dollies are not suitable for stairs, confirm movers’ equipment requirements (stair climber, appliance dolly, straps).

Steps to coordinate with neighbors / residents
1. Notify immediate neighbors and impacted residents:
  - Give date/time, truck size, and expected curb blockage. Explain you’ll try to minimize disruption and provide a contact number for the move day.
  - Offer a courtesy (e.g., a short window of vehicle relocation help or a small parking swap suggestion).

2. Post a clear notice the day before and move day
  - Use a short sign on doors/elevators: “Move scheduled on [date], truck expected 9am–1pm. Please avoid parking in front of building between [time].”
  - If management or city approves, post temporary “No Parking” cones/signs.

3. Manage parking logistics
  - If neighbors use the spot you need, ask if they can relocate temporarily or suggest alternate nearby parking.
  - If unavoidable and neighbors won’t move, consider hiring a smaller crew to shuttle items or ask movers for short-term double-parking plans (subject to local laws).

Permits & street rules
- Contact your city’s parking/transportation department for temporary loading zone or no-parking permits if the truck will block a lane or occupy a marked space. Many cities allow short-term permits for moves; timelines vary.
- Ask the mover if they need permit details on file; often movers will handle the permit if you give them the authority and the payment.

Dolly use, stair carries, and protecting common areas
- Dollies are usually fine for hallway and landing use but are unsafe on stairs; stair carries require experienced movers with stair straps or stair‑climber dollies.
- Agree with management on protective measures for hallways and stairs:
  - Floor runners, plywood sheets on vulnerable flooring, corner guards on walls, and padded wrapping on banisters.
  - Movers should use moving blankets, tape protectors, and soft straps — confirm they’ll bring those.
- If you must use a building dolly, inspect it first and confirm weight limits and condition.
- If building staff help with dolly movement, clarify liability and if they need to sign on or be present.

Insurance, liability, and damage protocol
- Provide management with movers’ insurance certificate if requested. Confirm what the mover’s coverage includes (liability for building damage and item damage).
- Ask your building how they handle claims for damage during moves and whether you need to sign a liability waiver or provide a refundable deposit.

Move‑day communications & logistics
- Give movers the building contact and management phone number in advance.
- Have the management contact and your phone number on hand for move day.
- Remind movers (and tell neighbors) the time window and that plants/pets will be handled separately.
- Confirm exact truck placement and an alternate spot if first choice is unavailable.

Sample short email to building management
Subject: Move Request — [Your Name], Apt #[#] — [Date]
Text to adapt:
Hi [Name], I’m scheduled to move into Apt #[#] on [date]. Move time window: [start]–[end]. The moving truck will be approximately [length] ft, with [#] movers. We’ll need curbside loading in front of the building and use of common-area hallways/stairs. Please let me know: any building rules, required permits/fees, whether the building can provide floor/banister protection or a dolly, and if you require proof of movers’ insurance. I can provide movers’ insurance details and will follow any building procedures. Thanks — [Your name, phone].

Sample neighbor note (door hanger or short message)
Hi neighbor — I’m moving into Apt #[#] on [date]. A moving truck may block the curb outside for a few hours between [time] and [time]. Sorry for any inconvenience — I’ll minimize disruption. Contact me at [phone] if this causes a problem. Thanks — [Name].

Practical tips & small contingencies
- If moving in a busy area or limited parking, reserve two contiguous spaces if allowed (one for truck, one for vehicle staging).
- Have a printed copy of building approval and city permit in the truck so parking enforcement or police can see it.
- Offer a small thank-you (water/snack or a quick favor) to neighbors you inconvenience — small gestures go a long way.
- Prepare a backup plan in case your reserved curb spot is taken: alternate parking spots, a short shuttle run, or a second smaller vehicle.
- If stairs are narrow, ask movers to test-carry the sectional orientation before attempting the full maneuver, and confirm they’ll pad and protect all touch points.

If you want, I can draft the exact short message for your building manager and for neighbors using the specific date/time and truck length you’ll use.

user: Do you have a simple labelling system recommendation for packing that helps when unpacking in the first week? | assistant: Yes — keep it simple, consistent, and visible. Use a short, repeatable format on each box, combine a visual cue (color/sticker) with a short text label, and keep one master inventory list. Practical system that’s fast to do and very helpful when unpacking:

1) Label format (write this on two adjacent sides + top)
- [ROOM CODE] • Box # • [SHORT CONTENT] • PRIORITY
  - Example: LR • 12 • Lamps / Cords • P1
  - Room codes: BR (bedroom), LR (living room), KT (kitchen), BA (bath), EN (entry), OF (office), MISC
- Priority: P1 = open first (essentials), P2 = next (important), P3 = last (nonessentials)

2) Add quick flags for special handling
- FRAGILE — big letters or a red sticker
- HEAVY — write HEAVY and use a yellow sticker
- OPEN FIRST — green sticker for essentials box(es)
- THIS SIDE UP — arrow(s) if orientation matters

3) Color-code by room (fast visual sorting)
- Use colored tape or round stickers (one color per room). Example:
  - Kitchen = blue, Bedroom = green, Living room = yellow, Bathroom = purple, Misc = orange
- Put one color sticker on two sides + top for quick identification.

4) Numbering + master inventory
- Number boxes sequentially (Box 1, Box 2, …). Keep a master list (paper or simple spreadsheet) that maps box # → detailed contents.
  - Example row: Box 12 | LR | Lamps, chargers, extension cords | P1 | fragile
- Update the master list as you pack; keep it on your phone and print a copy for moving day.

5) Essentials & valuables
- Use “OPEN FIRST” and P1 for your first‑night tote and pet supplies. Keep these with you, not on the truck.
- Mark valuables “Do not pack on truck” if you’ll carry them with you.

6) Placement & visibility tips
- Label at least two adjacent sides plus the top so you can read it regardless of how boxes are stacked.
- Use a permanent marker or a label maker. Cover paper labels with clear packing tape to prevent smearing.
- For very heavy items, add a weight note (e.g., ~30 lb) to avoid surprises.

7) Unpacking order guideline
- Stage by priority: P1 boxes (bedroom basics, toiletries, cat/livestock/pet kit, basic kitchen) → P2 (kitchenware, clothes) → P3 (books, décor).
- Unload boxes of the room you’re setting up first (bedroom and kitchen often come first).

8) Optional quick extras
- Photograph each packed box’s contents (snap a quick photo and link it to the box # in your master list) — useful for quick searches.
- If you have many fragile items, number plates/glassware boxes and list major items inside the box on the master sheet.

This system is fast to apply while packing and makes the first week of unpacking far less chaotic.

user: Friends will probably help for a day — what is a fair way to compensate them (cash, pizza, beer), and how many helpers do I realistically need for a third-floor walk-up? | assistant: Short version: plan on 3–4 strong helpers for the heavy lifts (sectional, fridge, bed) plus 1 person to drive/operate the truck — so 4–5 people total. Compensate based on time and effort: pizza/drinks for short help, modest cash or gift card plus food for a half day, and a larger cash amount + food/gas for a full, hard-moving day. Below are realistic guidance and sample packages.

How many helpers you’ll need
- Heavy single items (86" sectional, fridge on 3rd‑floor walk‑up): 3–4 strong people for each heavy carry (two to three to lift, one to spot/clear the route). If sectional disassembles, 2–3 may suffice for pieces.
- Boxes and general loading/unloading: 2–4 people to keep a steady flow between truck and apartment.
- Truck driver: someone to drive/park/secure the vehicle — often separate from the lifters.
- Total realistic crew: aim for 4–6 helpers (3–4 lifters + 1–2 box runners/driver). If you can only get 2–3 friends, hire 1–2 local laborers for the heavy items.

Roles to assign (so friends know what to expect)
- Heavy lifters (stairs & big pieces)
- Box runners (carry bundles of boxes up/down)
- Spotter / door person (protect walls, open doors, direct moves)
- Truck driver/loader (stacks items safely in truck)
- Pet/plant manager or someone to handle logistics/refreshments

Compensation guidelines (fair and simple)
- Short/occasional help (2–3 hours, light lifting): pizza + drinks, or $10–20 each.
- Half‑day (4–6 hours, moderate work): pizza + $25–50 cash/gift card each (or $10–15/hr equivalent).
- Full hard day (6–10 hours, heavy stair work): $50–100+ cash each plus food/soft drinks and gas reimbursement.
- Extras: cover parking/permit fees, pay for gas or rideshares if they drove, and provide a clear tip/bonus if the work was especially heavy or they stayed late.

Payment method & timing
- Ask their preference (cash, Venmo, PayPal, gift card) when confirming.
- Pay immediately on move day (cash or instant transfer) or right after unloading.
- If you promise food/beer, provide substantial nonalcoholic hydration and food BEFORE/ DURING the move — and wait to hand out alcoholic drinks until after all heavy work is done.

Etiquette & safety
- Be explicit in the invite: tell them start time, expected hours, physical difficulty, stair count, and what you’ll provide (gloves, water, lunch).
- Provide gloves, moving straps, and an appliance dolly if you can. Ask them not to lift if injured or unsure.
- Don’t push alcohol until the work is done; safety first.
- Offer to cover any minor injuries’ medical costs or have basic first aid on hand; encourage people to opt out of lifts if uncomfortable.
- If the sectional/fridge looks unsafe to move with friends, be ready to hire pros for those items — offer to reimburse helpers for time if plans change.

Example offers you can make when asking friends
- “Hey — moving day is Sat 9am–3pm. I’ll provide lunch, snacks, water, and $40 cash (or Venmo) for about 4 hours of help. I really need 3–4 strong people for stairs/sofa/fridge. Can you make it?”
- Or, for lighter help: “2–3 hours of box moving — pizza and beer after — thanks!”

If you want, I can draft a short message you can send friends that states roles, time, compensation, and what to bring.
